summary:
The Zulu Kingdom (c. 1816–1897 AD) was the most powerful and expansionist Nguni-speaking state in southern Africa, founded by Shaka Zulu around 1816–1818 through military conquest that revolutionized warfare across the region, and subsequently the last major indigenous African state to successfully defeat a full British colonial army in a set-piece battle before its ultimate absorption into the British Empire. Shaka (c. 1787–1828), who seized control of the small Zulu clan and transformed it into an empire through military genius, is best known for tactical and weapons innovations: replacing the throwing spear with a larger, heavier short-stabbing spear (assegai/iklwa) for close combat, introducing large ox-hide shields, and developing the 'bull horn' formation (encirclement tactic) that proved highly effective against neighboring peoples.

The Mfecane ('crushing,' also called Difaqane in Sotho) — the wave of violence, displacement, and state formation triggered by Zulu military expansion across southern Africa in the 1820s–1830s — reshaped the entire region: some peoples were destroyed; others were incorporated into the Zulu state; still others fled to found new states (Lesotho, Swaziland, Mzilikazi's Ndebele) far afield. By mid-century the Zulu Kingdom was the most powerful state between the Limpopo and the Indian Ocean. Under King Cetshwayo (r. 1873–1879), the Zulus famously destroyed a British column at the [[event:105|Battle of Isandlwana]] (January 22, 1879) — the greatest British military defeat at the hands of an indigenous African force — killing over 1,300 British and colonial troops. Despite this victory, the Zulus were ultimately defeated later that year at Ulundi, and the kingdom was divided and eventually annexed.
